questions.questionText: Your warehouse shipment-auditing agent, built on the Claude Agent SDK, calls `fetch_manifest`, `compare_records`, and `flag_discrepancy` in a loop. The loop terminates as soon as `flag_discrepancy` returns a success status. In production, 30% of manifests contain more than one discrepancy, and for those manifests the audit consistently records only the first discrepancy before stopping. What change fixes the premature termination?

options.A: Continue the loop while `stop_reason` is `"tool_use"` and terminate only when the model returns `"end_turn"`, regardless of which individual tools have succeeded along the way.
options.B: Have `flag_discrepancy` return a `remaining_discrepancies` count in its result and keep the loop running until that count reaches zero.
options.C: Add a system prompt instruction that the agent must output the phrase "AUDIT COMPLETE" only after flagging every discrepancy, and terminate the loop when that phrase appears.
options.D: Raise the loop's iteration cap so that manifests with multiple discrepancies have enough tool-call budget to flag all of them before the loop is cut off.

questions.explanation: The loop is treating one tool's success as task completion, but only the model knows whether its audit is finished; `stop_reason: "end_turn"` is the authoritative completion signal, and `"tool_use"` means keep going. Option B is programmatic, which is the right category, but it relocates termination knowledge into a tool that has no way to know how many discrepancies the model hasn't yet examined. Option C replaces one unreliable signal with another — parsing natural-language phrases for loop termination is a named anti-pattern because phrasing drifts across responses and prompt revisions. Option D misreads the failure: the 30% of multi-discrepancy audits are stopped by the success-status check, not by an exhausted iteration cap, so more budget changes nothing.

questions.questionText: A compliance agent drafts regulatory summaries after several tool calls. In about 4% of runs the final summary stops mid-sentence, yet the pipeline marks the task complete. Logs show those runs ended with `stop_reason: "max_tokens"`, and the loop's exit condition is "the response contains no `tool_use` blocks." What is the correct fix?

options.A: Treat only `stop_reason: "end_turn"` as completion; when the loop sees `"max_tokens"`, continue the conversation so the model can finish the truncated summary.
options.B: Raise `max_tokens` to the model's maximum for the summarization call, which removes the possibility of the response being cut off mid-sentence.
options.C: Detect truncation by checking whether the response's final text block ends in terminal punctuation, and retry the request whenever it does not.
options.D: When the loop sees `stop_reason: "max_tokens"`, discard the partial output and restart the task from the beginning with the original prompt.

questions.explanation: The exit condition conflates "no tool_use blocks" with "done" — a truncated response also contains no tool_use blocks, which is exactly why 4% of runs are marked complete mid-sentence; branching on `stop_reason` distinguishes genuine completion (`"end_turn"`) from truncation (`"max_tokens"`) and lets the model continue where it stopped. Option B shrinks the window but doesn't close it — long summaries can still hit the higher ceiling, and the loop would still misclassify those runs as complete. Option C is the natural-language-signal anti-pattern: a truncated summary can coincidentally end at a period, and a complete one can end without one. Option D diagnoses the stop reason correctly but recovers wastefully — restarting from scratch re-spends every prior token and, since nothing changed, may simply truncate again, whereas continuing the conversation completes the remaining portion only.

questions.questionText: A property-listing enrichment agent calls `geocode_address`, `fetch_school_ratings`, and `fetch_crime_stats` for each listing. To cut token spend, the developer's loop sends only the original instructions plus the single most recent tool result on each iteration, discarding earlier results. Listings that need all three lookups now trigger repeated calls to tools that already ran, and sessions average 11 tool calls where 3 should suffice. What change fixes this?

options.A: Accumulate the full conversation history across iterations — each assistant `tool_use` turn followed by its `tool_result` — so the model can see which lookups it has already completed and reason across their results.
options.B: Add a system prompt rule stating "Never call a tool you have already called for the current listing" so the model stops issuing redundant requests.
options.C: Deduplicate at the executor layer: when the model repeats a call with identical arguments, return the cached earlier result instead of re-executing the lookup against the backend.
options.D: Tighten the iteration cap to 5 tool calls per listing so redundant sessions are cut off early, capping the cost of the repeated lookups.

questions.explanation: The agentic loop depends on tool results persisting in conversation history; because each iteration exposes only the latest result, the model literally cannot know which lookups already ran, so it re-requests them — accumulating history restores that knowledge and drops sessions back toward 3 calls. Option B instructs the model to avoid something it has no information to detect: the record of prior calls was discarded, so the rule is unenforceable regardless of prompt strength. Option C cuts backend execution cost, which is real but partial — the model still sees only one result per iteration, so it still can't reason across the three datasets or recognize completion, and the 11-call loop persists. Option D treats the symptom by truncating it: listings needing all three lookups would now routinely terminate incomplete rather than cheaply.

questions.questionText: A trip-planning agent regularly emits a single assistant turn containing three `tool_use` blocks — `search_flights`, `search_hotels`, and `search_transfers`. Your loop executes only the first block and sends back one `tool_result`. The next API call then fails with a 400 error citing missing `tool_result` blocks. What is the correct handling?

options.A: Execute every `tool_use` block from the assistant turn and return one `tool_result` per `tool_use` ID, all inside the single next user message.
options.B: Return the flight result immediately, then supply the hotel and transfer results in two subsequent user messages as each remaining search completes.
options.C: Set `disable_parallel_tool_use` on the request's `tool_choice` so the model is constrained to requesting one tool per assistant turn.
options.D: Add a system prompt instruction telling the agent to call at most one tool per response so the loop's single-result assumption holds.

questions.explanation: Parallel tool use is valid API output: when an assistant turn contains multiple `tool_use` blocks, the immediately following user message must contain a matching `tool_result` for every block's ID, which is what option A implements — and the three independent searches then run in one round trip. Option B violates the protocol that produced the 400 in the first place: the results cannot be spread across later messages; they all belong in the next user message. Option C uses a real parameter and would deterministically stop the errors, but it fixes the model to accommodate a broken loop — turning one round trip into three for independent lookups and leaving the loop unable to handle valid output if the setting is ever removed. Option D is the probabilistic version of C: the model will sometimes still batch calls, and every time it does, the 400 returns.

questions.questionText: Your catalog-onboarding system uses a coordinator that delegates to three subagents: image tagging, description writing, and price validation. A teammate prototypes letting the price-validation subagent invoke the image-tagging subagent directly whenever it detects a photo/price mismatch, skipping the coordinator round trip and cutting that path's latency by 15%. How should you evaluate this proposal?

options.A: Reject it: direct subagent-to-subagent invocation fragments orchestration — the coordinator can no longer observe, sequence, or retry tagging work it never knew was delegated.
options.B: Approve it: the coordinator still receives both subagents' final outputs at the end of the run, so observability is preserved and the 15% latency gain comes at no architectural cost.
options.C: Reject it: routing the tagging output through the price agent's context roughly doubles token consumption compared with routing the same work through the coordinator.
options.D: Approve it on the condition that the price-validation subagent appends the full tagging transcript to its final report, restoring the coordinator's view of what happened.

questions.explanation: The hub-and-spoke pattern exists so one place holds delegation state: when subagents invoke each other directly, a failed or incomplete tagging call happens outside the coordinator's knowledge, so nothing can detect it mid-run, retry it, or account for it when sequencing the remaining work — a 15% latency gain on one path doesn't offset losing recovery over that path entirely. Option B mistakes receiving final outputs for orchestration: results arriving at the end doesn't help the coordinator manage failures while the run is in progress. Option C rejects for an inaccurate reason — coordinator-routed work also passes results through two agent contexts, so token cost is comparable; the real cost is control, not tokens. Option D is a near-miss: an appended transcript gives after-the-fact visibility, but reading about a delegation later is not the same as being able to sequence or recover it, and it bloats the price agent's report besides.

questions.questionText: A legal-review system analyzes 90-page merger agreements. The coordinator splits each document among three clause-analysis subagents by page range (1–30, 31–60, 61–90). In roughly 20% of runs, subagents misinterpret clauses that reference the 4-page defined-terms section at the front of the document — for example, page 70 uses "Material Adverse Effect" as defined on page 5, and the third subagent applies the plain-English meaning instead. What is the most effective fix?

options.A: Give every subagent the full 90-page document as context and instruct each one to analyze and report only on its assigned page range.
options.B: Decompose along self-contained semantic boundaries (articles or clause topics) instead of page ranges, and include the 4-page defined-terms section in every subagent's context.
options.C: Add a synthesis subagent that receives all three analyses and reconciles any conclusions that conflict with each other before the final report is produced.
options.D: Run the three subagents sequentially instead of in parallel, so each one can read the analyses produced by the subagents that ran before it.

questions.explanation: The failure is a decomposition that severs semantic dependencies: page ranges split content from the definitions it relies on. Option B fixes both halves — boundaries that keep dependent content together, plus the small shared dependency (4 pages) replicated into each subagent's context at negligible cost. Option A also restores correctness but at the wrong scale: it triples per-subagent input to 90 pages each when the actual shared dependency is only the 4-page definitions section, largely defeating the point of decomposing. Option C patches downstream — a synthesis pass can only reconcile conclusions the subagents surfaced, and a subagent that silently applied the plain-English meaning produces no conflict to detect. Option D sacrifices parallelism without solving the problem: the second and third subagents see prior analyses, not the defined-terms section, unless an earlier agent happened to quote it.

questions.questionText: An insurance-claims triage coordinator routes claims to specialist subagents (fraud screening, damage assessment, payout calculation), but performs damage assessment itself for claims it judges "simple," reasoning that a subagent round trip is wasteful. In long sessions, after roughly 40 claims, the coordinator's routing decisions degrade: it misroutes claim types it handled correctly earlier in the session. What architectural change best addresses this?

options.A: Enable context compaction on the coordinator's session so older claim analyses are summarized automatically once the context approaches its limit.
options.B: Replace the coordinator every 10 claims with a freshly spawned instance, handing off a summary of routing decisions made so far in the session.
options.C: Delegate every assessment — including simple ones — to subagents, keeping the coordinator's context reserved for orchestration state rather than analysis transcripts.
options.D: Add a system prompt instruction directing the coordinator to keep its own damage assessments brief so they consume less of the session's context.

questions.explanation: The coordinator is degrading because its context fills with damage-assessment working material — photos descriptions, line-item reasoning — that crowds out the orchestration state routing depends on; around claim 40, earlier routing patterns are effectively displaced. Option C fixes the cause: a thin coordinator whose context holds only task state scales with the number of claims, not the depth of each analysis. Option A treats the symptom — compaction buys more claims per session, but analysis transcripts still dilute the context, and lossy summaries of routing history are exactly what you don't want compacted. Option B is the same symptom-treatment with extra failure surface: the handoff summary must capture everything routing depends on, and the mixed-concern design that caused the bloat carries over to each new instance. Option D reduces the leak rate rather than plugging it — briefer assessments still accumulate, and it relies on probabilistic compliance besides.

questions.questionText: A restaurant-inspection platform generates compliance reports using three subagents: photo analysis, violation-history retrieval, and report writing. To maximize throughput, the coordinator spawns all three in parallel. Reports frequently ship missing photo findings, because the report writer starts before photo analysis — the slowest of the three at about 30 seconds — has returned. How should the coordinator schedule the subagents?

options.A: Keep all three parallel, but have the report writer retry in a loop, re-checking for photo findings every few seconds until they become available.
options.B: Run all three subagents strictly sequentially — photo analysis, then violation history, then report writing — so every dependency is guaranteed to be satisfied.
options.C: Keep all three parallel, and instruct the report writer to leave placeholder sections for photo findings that a second-pass subagent fills in once analysis completes.
options.D: Spawn photo analysis and violation-history retrieval in parallel, then invoke the report writer only after both have returned, passing it their outputs.

questions.explanation: Parallelism is for independent tasks; the report writer consumes the other two subagents' outputs, so it belongs after them. Option D preserves the real available concurrency — photo analysis and history retrieval don't depend on each other — while making the dependent step wait for its inputs, bounding total latency near the 30-second slowest branch. Option B is correct but over-serialized: it forfeits the parallelism between the two independent tasks and adds their durations for no benefit. Option A bolts a polling loop onto a scheduling problem — subagents don't share live state to poll, and even simulated via re-invocation it burns tokens waiting instead of just sequencing the dependency. Option C ships the race condition into the artifact: a placeholder-and-patch second pass adds a full extra invocation per report and risks stitched-in findings that contradict the surrounding text the writer produced without them.

questions.questionText: A payroll-migration coordinator needs a subagent to map fields between a legacy HR schema and a new provider's schema. The developer passes the coordinator's entire 60,000-token session transcript to the subagent — including lengthy vendor-contract negotiations unrelated to schema work. The subagent's mappings are correct but slow to produce, and it occasionally cites contract terms as if they were mapping constraints. What is the correct way to pass context here?

options.A: Build a focused task prompt for the subagent containing only the two schema definitions, the mapping requirements, and explicit acceptance criteria — not the coordinator's transcript.
options.B: Pass the full transcript but prepend an instruction telling the subagent to disregard any content not related to schema mapping when producing its output.
options.C: Summarize the 60,000-token transcript down to a few thousand tokens with a separate compression call, and pass the summary as the subagent's context.
options.D: Configure the subagent with a larger context window so the full transcript fits with ample headroom and no longer degrades its processing.

questions.explanation: Subagents start fresh, which is an advantage: the coordinator should curate exactly the inputs the task needs — schemas, requirements, success criteria — rather than exporting its own history. Option A eliminates both symptoms at the source: less input means faster processing, and absent contract text can't be miscited as a constraint. Option B leaves the distraction in context and asks the model to probabilistically ignore it — the observed miscitations are evidence that irrelevant content in context influences output regardless of instructions. Option C is better than the raw transcript but still the wrong operation: blanket compression of mostly irrelevant material may lossily mangle the schema details that actually matter while still carrying negotiation residue in. Option D solves a problem the scenario doesn't have — the 60,000 tokens already fit; the failure is relevance and dilution, not capacity.

questions.questionText: A retail-analytics coordinator spawns one inventory-audit subagent per store, all at once, for a 25-store chain. Under the account's rate limits — which comfortably sustain about 5 concurrent subagents — roughly 40% of the spawned subagents fail with 429 errors, and the nightly audit completes with gaps. How should the spawning be redesigned?

options.A: Switch to fully sequential spawning: run one store's audit to completion before starting the next, eliminating concurrent requests entirely.
options.B: Keep spawning all 25 at once, but add exponential-backoff retries to each subagent so that those receiving 429 responses eventually succeed.
options.C: Run the subagents through a bounded worker pool sized to the rate limit — about 5 concurrent — queuing the remaining stores until a slot frees up.
options.D: Ask the API provider for a rate-limit increase sized to 25 concurrent subagents, keeping the current spawn-everything-at-once design unchanged.

questions.explanation: The stated limit — about 5 sustainable concurrent subagents — is the load-bearing constraint: correct design spawns up to the limit and queues the rest, getting maximum safe parallelism (roughly 5× faster than sequential) with no 429 storm. Option A eliminates the errors but ignores the same number: running one at a time when 5 are sustainable makes the nightly audit about five times longer than necessary. Option B keeps the thundering herd and merely retries into it — backoff is a good companion to a pool, but as the sole fix the 25-way contention persists and completion time becomes erratic. Option D outsources the fix: even if granted, the design fails again at the next constraint change (a 40-store expansion, a tightened quota), whereas a pool adapts by changing one number.

questions.questionText: A grant-writing platform's coordinator delegates literature scans to a subagent 6–8 times per proposal. Each delegation returns the subagent's complete working transcript — every search query, dead end, and reasoning step, roughly 15,000 tokens — which the coordinator ingests. By the fifth delegation the coordinator's context is dominated by scan transcripts and its proposal-drafting quality visibly drops. What should change about the context passing?

options.A: After ingesting each transcript, have the coordinator write a condensed summary note and rely on that note for later drafting instead of the raw transcript.
options.B: Have the subagent return only a structured findings report — sources, key claims, relevance notes — while its working transcript stays in its own context and is discarded on completion.
options.C: Merge the 6–8 scans into a single larger delegation per proposal, so only one transcript comes back to the coordinator instead of many.
options.D: Increase the coordinator's context budget so all of the proposal's scan transcripts fit with room to spare for drafting.

questions.explanation: The boundary between coordinator and subagent is exactly where working detail should be shed: the subagent's searches and dead ends served their purpose inside the subagent, and only distilled findings are useful upstream, so option B keeps ~15,000 tokens of process out of the coordinator per delegation. Option A summarizes too late — the coordinator has already ingested each full transcript by the time it condenses, so the context cost is paid and dilution has occurred. Option C batches the volume rather than reducing it: one delegation covering 6–8 scans returns one proportionally larger transcript. Option D is a capacity fix for a relevance problem — the drafting degradation comes from the coordinator's context being dominated by process noise, which a bigger window preserves rather than prevents.

questions.questionText: A localization team routes all product-manual translations through one long-lived subagent session, reused across documents "so it learns house style." QA finds terminology from earlier manuals leaking into later, unrelated ones — a drone manual used climbing-harness terms from the previous job — in roughly 1 in 10 documents. What is the correct spawning design?

options.A: Keep the shared session but begin every new document with an explicit instruction to disregard all previously translated documents.
options.B: Keep the shared session and compact it between documents so earlier manuals are reduced to summaries before the next job starts.
options.C: Keep the shared session but lower the sampling temperature for translation calls to reduce the model's tendency to borrow terms across documents.
options.D: Spawn a fresh subagent per document, passing the house-style guide and a per-domain glossary explicitly in each spawn's starting context.

questions.explanation: The contamination comes from prior documents physically sharing the context window; fresh spawning removes that channel entirely, and the one thing the reuse was preserving — house style — is better carried as an explicit artifact passed to every spawn, which option D does. Option A leaves the climbing-harness terminology in context and asks the model to probabilistically ignore it; the 1-in-10 leak rate is what that approach produces. Option B is lossy in the wrong direction — summaries of earlier manuals still carry their distinctive terms, so compaction shrinks the contamination without removing it. Option C reaches for a sampling knob to fix a context problem: lower temperature makes the model more deterministic about its context, which still contains the foreign terminology.

questions.questionText: A mortgage platform's intake agent collects and verifies applicant documents, then hands off to an underwriting agent that runs with restricted data access the intake agent must not share. In 30% of handoffs, the underwriting agent asks applicants to re-upload documents intake already verified. The handoff today is a single instruction: "continue processing application #4471." What fixes the redundant requests?

options.A: Hand off a structured state artifact — checklist of verified documents, extracted applicant facts, and outstanding items — that becomes the underwriting agent's starting context.
options.B: Give the underwriting agent the intake agent's full conversation transcript so that nothing collected or verified during intake can be lost in the handoff.
options.C: Have the underwriting agent query the document store at startup and infer verification status from which applicant files are present in it.
options.D: Merge intake and underwriting into a single agent handling applications end-to-end, so no handoff exists to lose state across.

questions.explanation: The underwriting agent re-requests documents because the handoff carries an application ID and nothing else; a structured state artifact transfers exactly the process state — what was verified, what remains — that the ID alone doesn't encode. Option B transfers the state buried in a long raw dialogue full of upload failures and small talk, forcing the underwriter to re-derive verification status from conversation flow, which is token-heavy and error-prone compared to a checklist. Option C infers the wrong thing: a file's presence in the store doesn't distinguish verified documents from ones uploaded and rejected, so verification status — the state that matters — is guessed. Option D removes the failure mode but violates the stated constraint that underwriting runs with restricted data access separate from intake.

questions.questionText: A travel-booking workflow's third step charges the customer via `capture_payment`. The payment provider times out on about 5% of bookings, and in some of those the charge actually succeeded server-side before the timeout; the agent's retry then double-charges — about 2% of all bookings. What is the correct enforcement fix?

options.A: Increase the client-side timeout so slow-but-successful captures have time to return before the retry logic ever triggers.
options.B: Prohibit retries on the payment step entirely and route every timed-out capture to a human operator for manual resolution.
options.C: Attach an idempotency key to each `capture_payment` call and have the retry path check charge status first, so a retry can never re-execute a completed capture.
options.D: Add a reconciliation job that scans the day's transactions for duplicate charges and automatically refunds the extra capture.

questions.explanation: The defect is that a retry is not distinguishable from a first attempt; idempotency keys plus a status check make retries structurally safe, eliminating double charges rather than reducing their odds. Option A shrinks the window but can't close it — some captures will exceed any finite timeout, and each one that does still double-charges. Option B is safe but surrenders automation for the whole 5% timeout population to fix the 2% that actually double-charge, turning a mostly transient fault into a standing manual queue. Option D is the right category (programmatic) applied after the harm: customers still see double charges and wait out the refund cycle, and reconciliation heuristics can miss legitimate same-day duplicate purchases.

questions.questionText: A nightly invoice pipeline has four fixed steps that never vary: fetch new invoices, validate them against purchase orders, post them to the ledger, and email exceptions to accounting. It is currently implemented as an autonomous agent told to "process tonight's invoices," choosing its own tool order — and twice this month it skipped the purchase-order validation step. How should this be architected?

options.A: Add few-shot examples to the agent's prompt demonstrating the correct four-step order across several representative nights.
options.B: Encode the four steps as a deterministic pipeline in orchestration code, invoking the model within steps that need judgment — like drafting exception emails — rather than for step ordering.
options.C: Keep the agent in control of ordering but add a final self-review instruction requiring it to confirm all four steps ran before ending its turn.
options.D: Split each step into its own specialized subagent and introduce a coordinator agent that decides the invocation order each night.

questions.explanation: When a workflow's structure is fixed and known in advance, control flow belongs in code and the model belongs inside the steps that need language intelligence; option B makes skipping validation impossible rather than unlikely. Option A improves the odds but keeps step ordering probabilistic — for a ledger-affecting sequence that already failed twice in a month, better odds is the wrong target. Option C relies on the same model that skipped the step to notice it skipped the step, and its check runs after the ledger post, when un-validated invoices have already been recorded. Option D adds agents while preserving the defect: a coordinator agent deciding order each night is still a probabilistic decision-maker performing what should be a hardcoded sequence.

questions.questionText: An expense agent must obtain finance-team approval before submitting reimbursements over $2,000. Today it asks for approval in the chat and proceeds on any affirmative reply; an audit found 7 cases where the requester themselves replied "approved" and the agent submitted. What is the correct enforcement design?

options.A: Rewrite the prompt to define exactly whose messages count as approval and instruct the agent to confirm the replier's finance-team role before proceeding.
options.B: Require approval messages to include the approver's employee ID, which the agent checks against the finance-team roster before submitting.
options.C: Add an audit hook that flags any reimbursement over $2,000 lacking an approval message, producing a weekly exception report for finance.
options.D: Pause the workflow programmatically at the threshold, and gate `submit_reimbursement` on an approval recorded through an authenticated channel that the orchestrator verifies before the tool can execute.

questions.explanation: The vulnerability is that approval is inferred from unauthenticated conversation text; the fix is to move it out of band — the workflow halts, a verified approver acts through an authenticated channel, and the orchestrator checks that recorded fact before the submission tool is executable. Option A asks the agent to verify identity from message content, which the conversation doesn't actually carry — the model cannot distinguish the requester typing "approved" from a finance member doing so. Option B adds a credential that is just more typable text: any requester who knows or guesses an ID passes the check. Option C is detection after the money has moved — a weekly report converts a preventable violation into a recurring cleanup task.

questions.questionText: Your HR-assistant agent forwards employee queries to a third-party analytics MCP tool for intent classification. Legal requires that employee names and ID numbers never reach the third party. Where must the redaction be enforced?

options.A: In a PreToolUse hook that scans and redacts the tool call's arguments before execution, blocking the call outright if redaction cannot be applied cleanly.
options.B: In a PostToolUse hook that strips names and ID numbers from the classification result before it re-enters the model's context window.
options.C: In the system prompt, instructing the agent to remove names and ID numbers when it constructs the input for the analytics tool call.
options.D: In the analytics tool's server code, dropping PII fields on receipt before the classification model processes them.

questions.explanation: The data crosses the trust boundary at the moment the tool call executes, so the only enforcement point that satisfies "never reaches the third party" is interception before execution — a PreToolUse hook that rewrites arguments deterministically, with a hard block as the fallback. Option B operates on the response: by the time a PostToolUse hook runs, the un-redacted arguments have already been transmitted. Option C makes a legal requirement probabilistic — the agent will usually comply, and a single miss is a reportable violation. Option D puts the control on the wrong side of the boundary twice over: the server belongs to the third party, so you can't modify it, and even if you could, the PII has already arrived there.

questions.questionText: A fleet-dispatch agent's PreToolUse hook blocks `assign_route` calls that would violate driver hour-limit rules. The hook blocks silently — the model receives nothing back. Logs show the agent re-issuing the identical blocked call up to 5 times, then ending its turn reporting that the assignment was made. What should the hook do instead when it blocks?

options.A: Terminate the session whenever a violating call is blocked, so a fresh session can restart the dispatch task from a clean state.
options.B: Continue blocking silently but emit a detailed entry to the operations log each time, so the dispatch team can review blocked assignments.
options.C: Return a structured denial into the conversation — which rule failed, the driver's actual hour figures, and what alternatives are permissible — so the model can adapt its next action.
options.D: Silently rewrite the call's arguments to the nearest hour-compliant driver and allow the modified call to execute in place of the original.

questions.explanation: A blocked call with no feedback is invisible to the model, which explains both symptoms: it retries the same call because nothing told it the call failed, then reports success because nothing told it otherwise; option C closes the loop so the agent can reassign intelligently. Option A is disproportionate — the task is recoverable (assign a different driver), and a fresh session re-derives everything only to hit the same silent wall. Option B gives humans observability while leaving the model exactly as blind as today; the retry loop and the false success report both persist. Option D executes something, but the model's world-model now diverges from reality — it believes its original assignment ran, so its subsequent reasoning and its report to dispatch describe a route that was never created.

questions.questionText: Compliance requires a complete, tamper-resistant record of every tool call your procurement agent makes — arguments and results — including calls to three third-party MCP servers you do not control. What is the correct capture mechanism?

options.A: Add a `log_audit` tool and a strict system prompt rule requiring the agent to call it after every other tool call, describing the call it just made.
options.B: Register PreToolUse and PostToolUse hooks that write every call's arguments and result to an append-only store, executing deterministically on each call.
options.C: Have each tool's MCP server log its own invocations internally, keeping the audit record adjacent to where each tool actually executes.
options.D: Reconstruct the record in a nightly job that parses saved session transcripts and extracts the tool calls and results they contain.

questions.explanation: Hooks sit in the invocation path itself, so they observe every call regardless of which server handles it, fire deterministically, and can write to an append-only store at the moment of action — all three compliance properties at once. Option A makes auditing probabilistic and self-reported: the agent can forget the follow-up call, and its description of its own arguments is a paraphrase, not a record. Option C is deterministic but structurally incomplete — the three third-party servers can't be modified, so the calls most in need of oversight are precisely the ones never logged. Option D is reconstruction rather than capture: transcripts are mutable session files, content can be lost to compaction before the nightly run, and a record assembled after the fact is not tamper-resistant.

questions.questionText: You add a PreToolUse security hook that screens shell commands against a denylist. It is registered without a matcher, so it runs on every tool call — including `Read` and `Grep` — adding roughly 250ms per call. Investigation sessions averaging 80 tool calls now take about 20 seconds longer, though only around 10% of those calls are actually `Bash`. What is the right fix?

options.A: Replace the hook with a system prompt section listing the forbidden shell commands, removing the interception overhead entirely.
options.B: Keep the hook global but cache screening verdicts keyed by tool arguments, so repeated identical calls skip the 250ms check.
options.C: Convert it to a PostToolUse hook, which runs off the pre-execution critical path and no longer delays each tool call.
options.D: Scope the hook with a matcher so it fires only on `Bash` tool calls, leaving the other ~90% of calls uninstrumented.

questions.explanation: The hook's coverage is wrong, not its existence: matchers exist precisely so an interception applies only to the tools it protects, and scoping to `Bash` keeps the deterministic security check while removing ~90% of the added latency. Option A trades a guaranteed control for a probabilistic one — a denylist the model is asked to respect is not a denylist. Option B mostly doesn't fire: investigation calls like `Read` and `Grep` take overwhelmingly unique arguments, so the cache misses and the overhead remains. Option C makes the check fast by making it useless — screening a shell command after it has executed inverts the purpose of a pre-execution security gate.

questions.questionText: You are documenting a data warehouse of 20 schemas totaling about 200 tables; individual schemas range from 3 to 45 tables. A first attempt decomposed the work into one subtask per column — roughly 3,000 subtasks. Coordination overhead dwarfed the actual writing, and the column docs read incoherently because each was written without its table's context. What decomposition fixes this?

options.A: Decompose per schema — 20 subtasks — so every writer holds the broadest possible context while it documents.
options.B: Keep the per-column subtasks but prepend each one with a generated summary of its parent table for coherence.
options.C: Decompose per table — about 200 subtasks — so each unit is written with its full table context and stays bounded in size regardless of schema.
options.D: Run it as a single sequential session over all 200 tables, relying on context compaction to manage the accumulated material.

questions.explanation: The table is the natural semantic unit here: columns only make sense in their table's context, and a table is small enough to document in one focused subtask, so 200 subtasks fixes both stated failures — coordination overhead collapses by ~15x and coherence comes free. Option A ignores the stated size spread: a 45-table schema is a very different subtask from a 3-table one, so per-schema units are unbounded and the large ones recreate a context problem. Option B patches coherence while leaving the dominant stated cost — coordinating 3,000 subtasks — completely intact, and adds a summary-generation step to every one of them. Option D trades decomposition for one enormous session in which compaction progressively discards earlier tables' details, degrading exactly the cross-referencing a warehouse doc needs.

questions.questionText: A campaign-launch system translates marketing copy into 12 languages. Past runs translated everything in parallel, and brand terms came out inconsistent across languages in about a quarter of campaigns — one language transliterated a product name that another left in English. How should the work be decomposed?

options.A: Two phases: first produce a canonical terminology list (which terms stay in English, which localize, and how), then run the 12 translations in parallel with that list in each subtask's context.
options.B: Keep the 12 parallel translations and add a reconciliation pass afterward that compares outputs across languages and corrects any terminology inconsistencies it detects.
options.C: Run the 12 translations sequentially, letting each language's subtask see the previous languages' finished output so terminology choices propagate down the chain.
options.D: Have each language's subtask independently derive terminology rules from the brand guidelines as its first step, before translating its assigned copy.

questions.explanation: The inconsistency exists because a shared decision — how to treat each brand term — is currently being made 12 times independently; sequencing that one decision before the fan-out makes it once, then the translations are genuinely independent and safely parallel. Option B allows the divergence and then pays to find it: cross-language inconsistency detection requires comparing 12 outputs pairwise in 12 languages, an expensive and unreliable rework pass for something a one-page artifact prevents. Option C buys consistency by serializing everything — roughly 12x the latency — and produces consistency by accretion, where every later language inherits whichever unvetted choice the first language happened to make. Option D is the current failure restated with an extra step: 12 independent derivations from the same guidelines are 12 opportunities to diverge, which is what the 25% figure already measures.

questions.questionText: You must migrate 60 REST endpoints from Express to Fastify; each endpoint has a route definition, a handler, and tests. The team requires CI to stay green on every merge throughout the migration. Which decomposition satisfies that?

options.A: By layer: migrate all 60 route definitions first, then all 60 handlers, then update all the tests as a final phase.
options.B: By endpoint: each subtask migrates one endpoint end-to-end — route, handler, and its tests — merging a working vertical slice each time.
options.C: As one large session that migrates the whole codebase in a single continuous effort, relying on compaction to manage the accumulated context.
options.D: As alphabetical batches of 15 files each, sized so every merge produces a diff small enough to review comfortably regardless of endpoint boundaries.

questions.explanation: Endpoint-level vertical slices are the only decomposition here whose intermediate states are complete: after every merge, each endpoint is either fully Express or fully Fastify with passing tests, so CI stays green throughout — the stated requirement. Option A's intermediate states are structurally broken: routes migrated ahead of handlers can't pass their tests, so CI is red for the entire middle of the migration. Option C abandons incremental verification entirely — one failure domain spanning 60 endpoints, with compaction eroding early-migration details the later work depends on. Option D optimizes a real but secondary concern: alphabetical file batches cut endpoints in half across merges (a route in one batch, its handler in another), recreating option A's broken intermediate states with tidier diffs.

questions.questionText: Yesterday a developer's Claude Code session started a local mock-payment server and set several test environment variables while debugging a checkout flow. This morning they run `--resume`, and the agent immediately fires requests at the mock server — which died overnight — then spends 15 minutes misdiagnosing the connection failures as checkout-code bugs. What is the correct understanding and fix?

options.A: Session state is designed to include background processes; configure the session so its processes persist across resumes and the morning failures disappear.
options.B: Use `fork_session` instead of `--resume`, since forking re-initializes the working environment while resuming carries yesterday's stale environment forward.
options.C: Add a CLAUDE.md rule forbidding the agent from depending on long-running local processes, so no session can be misled by a dead server again.
options.D: Resumption restores conversation history, not external state; start resumed sessions by re-establishing the environment — restart the mock server, re-verify env vars — before continuing the investigation.

questions.explanation: A resumed session gets its memory back, not its world: the conversation says a server is running, the machine disagrees, and the agent trusts its context — so the fix is an explicit re-establish-and-verify step at the top of any resumed session. Option A rests on a false premise: session persistence covers the conversation, and no configuration makes it keep operating-system processes alive overnight. Option B swaps in a real mechanism with an invented property — forking branches the conversation history from a point, and neither forking nor resuming touches the execution environment. Option C prevents the symptom by banning a legitimate practice: long-running processes are fine within a session; the failure is specific to trusting them across a resume without re-verification.

questions.questionText: A feature build will span about three weeks of daily Claude Code work. A teammate proposes carrying context forward by resuming one ever-growing session each morning. In a previous attempt at this, the session was compacting constantly by week two and the agent had lost track of early design decisions. What is the right session strategy?

options.A: Keep resuming the single session but compact manually at the end of each day, deliberately choosing which material survives into the next morning.
options.B: Fork the session each morning instead of resuming it, so every day begins on a clean branch of the previous day's state.
options.C: Externalize durable state into repository files — design decisions, a progress log, next steps — and run focused sessions that read those documents at start and update them at end.
options.D: Start a fresh session each morning and paste the prior day's full transcript into it as opening context, guaranteeing nothing is silently dropped.

questions.explanation: Three weeks of work will not fit in any context window, so the durable state must live outside the session: files carry decisions losslessly and indefinitely, each day's session stays small and focused, and week one's design rationale is as available in week three as the day it was written. Option A still funnels weeks of accumulating history through one lossy channel — manual curation slows the degradation the previous attempt saw but cannot arrest it. Option B misunderstands what forking does: the fork inherits the parent's full history, so each morning's branch starts exactly as bloated as the session it branched from. Option D re-pays the entire history as fresh input every morning, growing daily until the transcript itself no longer fits — the compaction problem reproduced by hand.

questions.questionText: A ride-sharing app has two tools: `estimate_fare` ("Returns a fare for a trip") and `calculate_trip_cost` ("Returns the cost of a trip"). Logs show that in 18% of completed trips, the agent calls `estimate_fare` after the ride has ended and displays the pre-trip estimate to the rider as their final charged amount, which sometimes differs from what was actually billed. What is the most effective fix?

options.A: Add few-shot examples to the system prompt showing the agent calling `calculate_trip_cost` after a trip ends and `estimate_fare` before one begins.
options.B: Rewrite both tools' descriptions to state an explicit boundary: `estimate_fare` returns a pre-trip quote only and should never be used once a trip has ended, while `calculate_trip_cost` returns the finalized post-trip amount — each description naming the other tool and when to defer to it.
options.C: Add a routing layer that classifies whether a trip is in progress or completed and calls the correct tool programmatically based on that classification.
options.D: Consolidate both tools into a single `get_fare_info` tool that always returns whatever amount is currently available, whether estimated or final.

questions.explanation: The two descriptions never establish a boundary, so the model has no way to learn from them which one applies to a finished trip; naming the boundary and cross-referencing the sibling tool in both descriptions is the direct fix, and it's a one-time change rather than new infrastructure. Option A leaves the ambiguous descriptions in place and hopes examples generalize to the many trip-timing edge cases 18% of trips already represent — a probabilistic patch on a documentation gap. Option C solves the same problem with new code most of a codebase doesn't need when the tools' own text can carry the distinction. Option D removes the very distinction riders need — a rider mid-trip and one who just finished would get indistinguishable responses from a single merged tool.

questions.questionText: A pharmacy app's `schedule_refill` tool requires a `refill_date` argument in `YYYY-MM-DD` format. Its description reads only: "Schedules a prescription refill for a specified date." Logs show the agent frequently passes phrases like "next Monday" or "in two weeks," and 22% of refill requests fail validation as a result. What change most directly reduces these failures?

options.A: Add a system prompt instruction: "Always call `schedule_refill` using YYYY-MM-DD format."
options.B: Add schema validation that rejects any `refill_date` not matching `YYYY-MM-DD` and returns a structured error back to the agent.
options.C: Add the required format, an example value, and a note that relative phrases like "next Monday" must be resolved to a calendar date before calling, directly into the tool's description.
options.D: Split `schedule_refill` into `schedule_refill_relative` and `schedule_refill_exact` to separate the two input styles.

questions.explanation: The tool description is the model's primary source of information at the moment it decides how to format a call, so putting the format, an example, and the relative-phrase caveat there addresses the failures at their origin. Option A adds the same instruction one layer removed, in the system prompt, which is more fragile than information attached directly to the tool the model is about to invoke. Option B would catch bad input after the fact and could reduce silent errors, but it doesn't teach the model the correct format up front, so the 22% failure-then-retry cycle continues rather than being prevented. Option D solves a disambiguation problem this scenario doesn't have — there's only one date argument and one failure mode (wrong format), not two competing use cases that need separate tools.

questions.questionText: A city permitting system exposes three tools: `get_permit_status` ("Get the status of a permit"), `check_permit_details` ("Check details for a permit"), and `retrieve_application_record` ("Retrieve a record for an application"). Each calls a different backend — building department, zoning department, and code enforcement respectively — and the agent frequently queries the wrong department's system for a given case. What most effectively resolves the misrouting?

options.A: Add a system prompt instruction listing all three tools and specifying which department each is used for.
options.B: Consolidate the three tools into a single tool that accepts a `department` parameter specifying which backend to query.
options.C: Have the agent call all three tools for every request and merge whichever results come back non-empty.
options.D: Rename each tool to reflect its backend (e.g. `get_building_permit_status`, `get_zoning_case_details`, `get_code_enforcement_record`) and rewrite each description to state what data it returns, which department it queries, and when to use it.

questions.explanation: The three names and descriptions are currently near-synonyms that give the model no way to distinguish them, so renaming to reflect the actual backend and writing descriptions around what each returns and when it applies fixes the root ambiguity. Option A layers guidance in the system prompt on top of tools whose own names and descriptions still look interchangeable, leaving the underlying confusion in place for any request not explicitly covered by that list. Option B trades three ambiguous tools for one tool whose correctness now depends on the model picking the right `department` value — the same discrimination problem, just moved into a parameter instead of a tool name. Option C isn't a real fix; querying all three departments for every request wastes calls and still requires deciding which result is the relevant one.

questions.questionText: An e-commerce catalog exposes `search_products` (keyword search only) and `filter_products` (structured attribute and price filtering). `search_products`'s description doesn't mention that it ignores price constraints. In roughly 20% of complex queries — e.g. "waterproof boots under $80" — the agent passes the price as part of the search keywords to `search_products`, which silently ignores it and returns boots across all price ranges. What is the most effective fix?

options.A: Rewrite `search_products`'s description to state explicitly that it performs keyword matching only, that it does not apply price or attribute constraints, and that `filter_products` should be used for those, with an example of a query that needs both tools together.
options.B: Merge `search_products` and `filter_products` into a single tool that accepts both a keyword string and structured filter parameters.
options.C: Add few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating a combined keyword-and-price query handled correctly.
options.D: Add a validation layer to `search_products` that detects price-like values in the keyword string and returns an error asking the agent to retry using `filter_products` instead.

questions.explanation: The failure is that `search_products`'s description never discloses its own boundary, so the model has no way to know price constraints need a different tool; stating the boundary and pointing to the sibling tool, with a worked example, fixes the ambiguity at its source. Option B is a legitimate architectural response to overlapping tools, but it's a heavier redesign than the ambiguity warrants when a description fix resolves it directly — and it complicates the schema for the 80% of queries that only need keyword search. Option C leaves the description's silent gap in place and asks examples to generalize across combined-query phrasing, which is exactly the kind of probabilistic patch that a boundary statement in the description avoids needing. Option D catches the mistake only after the call is made and burns a round trip on every offending query, rather than preventing the agent from choosing the wrong tool in the first place.

questions.questionText: An HR system has `update_employee_record` for general field updates and `terminate_employment`, which enforces a manager-approval workflow. `update_employee_record`'s description is a long paragraph that mentions, midway through, "...including status changes such as active, leave, or terminated." In 9% of terminations, the agent sets status to "terminated" via `update_employee_record`, bypassing the approval workflow entirely. What is the most effective fix?

options.A: Add a `PreToolUse` hook that intercepts `update_employee_record` calls and blocks any that attempt to set status to "terminated."
options.B: Rewrite `update_employee_record`'s description to lead with an explicit exclusion — stating up front that it must never be used to set status to "terminated," and directing the model to `terminate_employment` for that action — rather than burying the carve-out mid-paragraph.
options.C: Add a system prompt rule instructing the agent to never use `update_employee_record` for terminations.
options.D: Add few-shot examples showing `terminate_employment` used for every termination scenario in the prompt.

questions.explanation: The description technically documents the overlap but buries the one detail that matters where the model is least likely to weight it; moving the exclusion to the front of the description is a direct interface fix for a tool-selection failure rooted in how the tool is described. Option A is a real, deterministic safeguard for the approval workflow itself, but it treats the symptom of this specific misuse rather than the tool-design defect that let a poorly-documented boundary cause it, and it does nothing for other undocumented edge cases the same buried-detail pattern could still produce. Option C repeats the same probabilistic reliance on prompt compliance the buried clause already represents, just relocated to a different document the model must also weigh correctly. Option D show the right tool being used correctly but doesn't address why the wrong tool is chosen in the first place — the model isn't failing to know `terminate_employment` exists, it's failing to notice `update_employee_record`'s hidden exclusion.

questions.questionText: A food-delivery platform's `assign_driver` MCP tool returns the same generic response for every failure: `{"success": false}`. This single shape is triggered by: no drivers currently available, the target driver already has an active delivery, an invalid restaurant address, and a declined payment authorization. The agent currently responds to every case identically — apologizing to the customer and cancelling the order. What structured error design would most improve recovery?

options.A: Return a distinct HTTP status code for each of the four failure conditions and have the agent branch its recovery logic on the status code.
options.B: Return a verbose error payload including the full backend stack trace and driver-dispatch system state for every failure, giving the agent maximum context to reason from.
options.C: Return structured error metadata — an `errorCategory` (e.g. transient/validation/permission/business), an `isRetryable` boolean, and a explanation specific to the failure reason.
options.D: Return a numeric error code for each condition and maintain a mapping from code to recovery action in the system prompt.

questions.explanation: The four failures need genuinely different responses — retry shortly for no-drivers-available, ask for a corrected address, don't retry a declined payment — and structured category/retryability/explanation fields give the agent exactly the decision inputs it needs to tell them apart. Option A relies on HTTP status semantics that weren't designed to express business-logic distinctions like "driver already has an active delivery," and mapping four domain-specific conditions onto generic codes loses the nuance that fields like `isRetryable` capture directly. Option B floods the agent's context with backend internals it can't act on and that have no bearing on which of the four categories applies, without adding any decision-relevant structure. Option D encodes the actual recovery logic in the system prompt rather than in the tool's own response, which becomes harder to maintain as new failure conditions are added and keeps failure semantics separate from the data describing them.

questions.questionText: An inventory tool `check_stock` returns `{"available_locations": [], "status": "success"}` whenever it's called. Investigation reveals the inventory database was actually unreachable for several hours, but the tool's error handling caught the connection failure and returned an empty locations list marked successful. During that window, the agent told customers the item was "out of stock everywhere" rather than reporting a system issue. What change prevents this confusion?

options.A: Always return at least one location in the array, using cached or estimated data whenever the live lookup returns nothing.
options.B: Add a `confidence` field to the response so the agent can infer whether an empty result reflects a real outcome or a failure.
options.C: Implement automatic retry inside the tool itself, so the agent never observes an empty result unless every retry attempt is exhausted.
options.D: Distinguish access failures from valid empty results: use the `isError` flag when the database can't be reached, and reserve `{"available_locations": [], "status": "success"}` only for genuine, confirmed no-stock-anywhere outcomes.

questions.explanation: The tool is collapsing two fundamentally different conditions — the backend being unreachable, and the backend confirming there's genuinely no stock — into an identical response, so option D's `isError` distinction gives the agent the information needed to report a system issue instead of a false stock-out. Option A fabricates plausible-looking data during an outage, which is worse than an ambiguous response because it presents guessed information as a confirmed inventory check. Option B adds a field whose meaning is genuinely unclear for a real, confirmed empty result — a confidence score on "we checked every location and found none" doesn't correspond to anything meaningful. Option C addresses retries at the wrong layer and doesn't fix the underlying confusion: after retries are exhausted the tool still returns the same misleading successful-empty shape, and the agent still can't tell an outage from a genuine stock-out.

questions.questionText: A content-moderation tool `publish_post` returns `{"isError": true, "message": "Blocked by moderation"}` when a post is flagged for a policy violation. The agent interprets this as transient and retries up to four times, and each retry generates a separate duplicate review ticket in the compliance queue. What is the missing element in the error response design?

options.A: Include `isRetryable: false` along with an explanation that distinguishes this policy-based block from a transient system error, so the agent stops retrying and instead surfaces the block appropriately.
options.B: Suppress the `isError` flag and instead return the block as a successful response with a `blocked: true` field.
options.C: Include a `retry_after` timestamp telling the agent when it would be appropriate to retry the call.
options.D: Include the specific moderation rule ID and a confidence score so the agent can decide for itself whether to override the block and republish.

questions.explanation: A policy block is inherently non-retryable — republishing the identical content will trigger the identical block — so `isRetryable: false` paired with a clear explanation is precisely the signal missing today, and it directly stops the duplicate-ticket-generating retry loop. Option B hides that a genuine error condition occurred behind a successful-looking response, which makes the block harder for calling code and monitoring to distinguish from ordinary success. Option C implies the block is temporary and will resolve with time, which is the opposite of what a policy violation represents — retrying after any elapsed period reproduces the same block. Option D hands the agent authority to second-guess and override a compliance decision it is very likely not meant to have — moderation blocks are a business rule constraint, not a judgment call for the requesting agent to relitigate.

questions.questionText: A `validate_address` MCP tool, on failure, returns the full exception stack trace, the internal database query it ran, and the backend server's hostname directly in the response sent to the model. This floods the agent's context on every failed validation, and on at least two occasions the agent has quoted the internal hostname back to a customer in an apology message. What is the right fix?

options.A: Keep the full diagnostic payload in the response but add a warning field instructing the agent not to repeat internal details to customers.
options.B: Return a concise structured error to the model — an error category, a customer-safe explanation, and a suggested corrective action — and log the full stack trace, query, and hostname server-side only, never in the tool's response.
options.C: Truncate the stack trace to only its first line before including it in the response.
options.D: Wrap the internal diagnostic details in a code block within the response so the agent recognizes them as technical rather than customer-facing.

questions.explanation: The internal details never needed to reach the model at all — they're useful only for engineers debugging server-side — so keeping them out of the tool's response entirely both removes the context bloat and makes it structurally impossible for the model to quote a hostname it was never given. Option A leaves the sensitive material in the model's context and asks it to reliably withhold specific pieces of it, which is exactly the kind of instruction-following the two prior incidents already show can fail. Option C reduces the volume of leaked internals without addressing that any of them reach the model at all — a truncated stack trace's first line can still contain a hostname or query fragment. Option D changes formatting, not exposure — the hostname is still present in the model's context and available for it to reproduce regardless of how it's visually delimited.

questions.questionText: A `bulk_import_customers` tool processes batches of up to 500 records. On a batch of 500 where 14 have invalid email addresses, the tool returns `{"status": "error"}` for the whole batch — but investigation shows it had already committed the other 486 valid records to the database before hitting the invalid ones. The agent, seeing only the generic error, resubmits all 500 records, creating duplicate entries for the 486 that already succeeded. What is the most effective fix?

options.A: Automatically roll back the 486 successful commits whenever any record in the batch fails validation, so the batch is always all-or-nothing.
options.B: Add stricter pre-validation that checks every record in the batch before committing any of them, so partial commits can never occur.
options.C: Return a structured partial-success response listing the count and identifiers of the committed records plus the specific reason each of the 14 failed, so the agent knows to resubmit only the failed subset.
options.D: Reduce the maximum batch size so each request contains fewer records, lowering the odds that any single batch contains an invalid record.

questions.explanation: The actual defect is that the response doesn't reflect what genuinely happened — 486 records did succeed — so reporting that partial outcome accurately is what lets the agent act correctly, resubmitting only the 14 that failed instead of all 500. Option A throws away 486 legitimate, already-committed imports purely to make the response shape simpler, which is a costly overcorrection when accurate partial reporting solves the same confusion without discarding real work. Option B would prevent this specific partial-commit shape, but it requires validating the entire batch atomically before any writes, which may not be architecturally available here and doesn't generalize to failures that only manifest during the commit itself. Option D shrinks batch size without changing what the tool reports when a failure does occur — smaller batches can still contain an invalid record and would still trigger the same misleading all-or-nothing error today.

questions.questionText: A caption-writing agent frequently needs to verify text length against platform limits while drafting. Today, every check round-trips through the coordinator to a specialized text-analysis agent, adding 2-3 exchanges per caption. About 90% of these checks are simple word or character counts; the remaining 10% require genuine readability analysis (sentence complexity, reading-grade level) that only the specialized agent can perform. What is the most effective way to reduce overhead while preserving reliability for the complex cases?

options.A: Give the caption-writing agent direct access to the specialized agent's full readability-analysis toolset, bypassing the coordinator for every check.
options.B: Have the caption-writing agent accumulate all its verification needs during a pass and send them to the coordinator as a single batch at the end.
options.C: Have the specialized readability agent proactively pre-compute anticipated checks for every caption during the initial drafting phase.
options.D: Give the caption-writing agent a scoped `count_words` tool to handle the common case directly, while complex readability analysis continues to be delegated to the specialized agent through the coordinator.

questions.explanation: Distributing only the narrowly-scoped tool the 90% case actually needs keeps the existing coordination pattern intact for the 10% that genuinely requires it, cutting most of the round trips without touching the path that needs specialist judgment. Option A over-provisions the caption agent with an entire specialized toolset when it only ever needs simple counts, increasing the chance it misuses tools meant for genuine readability analysis. Option B still funnels every check, including the trivial ones, through a coordinator round trip — just fewer, larger ones — and introduces a blocking delay where verification results aren't available until the whole pass finishes. Option C asks the specialized agent to guess which checks will be needed before drafting has happened, which is speculative work that may not match what the caption agent actually ends up needing to verify.

questions.questionText: An invoice-formatting agent's sole job is to lay out and style invoice documents. It has been given access to the full 15-tool suite used across the billing system, including `delete_invoice` and `send_payment_reminder`, on the reasoning that more tools provide more flexibility. On one occasion it called `send_payment_reminder` mid-formatting, sending a reminder to a customer who hadn't been billed yet. What is the most effective fix?

options.A: Scope the agent's tool access down to only what formatting requires, removing `delete_invoice`, `send_payment_reminder`, and other tools outside its specialization.
options.B: Add a system prompt instruction telling the agent to only use tools relevant to formatting invoices.
options.C: Keep the full 15-tool suite but require manager confirmation before `send_payment_reminder` is allowed to execute.
options.D: Reduce the suite to the 5 most commonly used tools instead of all 15, keeping the rest available on request.

questions.explanation: An agent given tools outside its specialization will occasionally misuse them regardless of intent, so scoping its tool set to formatting-only tools removes the capability that produced the misfire entirely, rather than managing the risk it creates. Option B leaves every unrelated tool, including `send_payment_reminder`, available and reachable, relying on the same probabilistic prompt compliance that already failed once. Option C adds a safeguard specifically around the one tool that already misfired but leaves the agent holding `delete_invoice` and the rest of the unrelated suite, so a different tool could misfire the same way next. Option D reduces the count without addressing relevance — nothing guarantees the 5 "most commonly used" tools are the formatting-relevant ones, and a mis-scoped tool used often is still mis-scoped.

questions.questionText: A legal-document review agent must call `redact_pii` on every single invocation before it finalizes its output — never optionally, never in place of some other tool. Which `tool_choice` configuration guarantees this?

options.A: Set `tool_choice: "auto"`, letting the model decide when redaction is needed.
options.B: Set `tool_choice: {"type": "tool", "name": "redact_pii"}`, forcing the model to call `redact_pii` specifically.
options.C: Set `tool_choice: "any"`, requiring the model to call some tool without specifying which one.
options.D: Remove every other tool from the agent's tool list so `redact_pii` is the only one it can call.

questions.explanation: Forcing the specific tool by name is the only configuration that guarantees both that a tool is called and that it's the one that matters here, on every single invocation. Option A leaves the decision to the model, which can return plain text with no tool call at all — the opposite of "never optionally." Option C guarantees a tool call happens but not which one, so if the agent has other legitimate tools available it could satisfy the constraint by calling one of those instead of `redact_pii`. Option D achieves the outcome indirectly by removing every alternative, but it strips the agent of any other tools it may legitimately need elsewhere in its workflow, rather than simply constraining this specific requirement.

questions.questionText: A signup flow requires that before an account is created, the agent perform at least one identity check — either `verify_email` or `verify_phone` — but it genuinely does not matter which one, and the agent should be free to pick based on what contact information the user provided. Which `tool_choice` configuration is correct?

options.A: Set `tool_choice: "auto"`, letting the model decide whether verification is needed at all.
options.B: Set `tool_choice: {"type": "tool", "name": "verify_email"}`, forcing the model to call `verify_email` specifically.
options.C: Set `tool_choice: "any"`, guaranteeing the model calls some tool without constraining which of the two verification tools it is.
options.D: Remove `verify_phone` from the agent's tool list so only `verify_email` remains available.

questions.explanation: "Any" is the configuration built for exactly this shape of requirement — it guarantees a tool call happens on every invocation while leaving the choice of which eligible tool to the model, matching the stated "doesn't matter which" constraint precisely. Option A doesn't guarantee a tool call at all; the model could proceed to account creation reasoning without having verified anything, which is the failure mode this requirement exists to prevent. Option B forces a specific one of the two, which over-constrains the requirement — a user who only provided a phone number would have no way to satisfy verification through the tool the model is being forced to call. Option D achieves single-tool forcing by removing the alternative, which permanently eliminates phone-based verification as an option for every user, not just the ones who happen to provide an email.

questions.questionText: A payment tool `charge_customer` is granted to both the intake agent and the billing agent in a multi-agent order pipeline, "just in case" either one needs it. In about 3% of orders, both agents independently decide the charge is due and call `charge_customer`, resulting in the customer being billed twice. What is the most effective fix?

options.A: Add an idempotency key to `charge_customer` so that a duplicate call doesn't result in a second charge.
options.B: Add a system prompt instruction to the intake agent telling it never to call `charge_customer`.
options.C: Merge the intake and billing agents into a single agent, so there is only one place `charge_customer` could ever be called from.
options.D: Remove `charge_customer` from the intake agent's tool list, distributing it only to the billing agent — the one actually responsible for that step.

questions.explanation: Granting the same consequential tool to two agents for the same step is a distribution error independent of whether double-charging happens to occur, and removing it from the agent that isn't responsible for billing eliminates the redundant authority at its source. Option A is a real, valid safeguard for retries of the *same* call, but it depends on both independently-constructed calls sharing an identical idempotency key, which isn't guaranteed across two separately reasoning agents — it treats a symptom that could recur in a different form even if this exact duplication is caught. Option B relies on the intake agent reliably following an instruction not to use a tool it still has access to, which is the same probabilistic compliance already shown to fail 3% of the time. Option C removes the redundancy but does so by eliminating the coordinator-subagent separation entirely, a far larger architectural change than the actual defect — one agent holding a tool it shouldn't — requires.

questions.questionText: Your team is setting up a shared Jira MCP server for everyone on a project. The server needs an API token for authentication. You want every engineer to get the server automatically when they clone the repository, without individually configuring it, and without the token ever being committed in plaintext. What is the correct approach?

options.A: Add the MCP server to the project-scoped `.mcp.json` file with the token specified via environment variable expansion (e.g. `${JIRA_TOKEN}`), and commit `.mcp.json` to the repository.
options.B: Add the MCP server to each developer's personal `~/.claude.json` with their own token hardcoded.
options.C: Document the required MCP server configuration in the README and have each developer add it manually to their own setup.
options.D: Commit `.mcp.json` with the token value embedded directly, relying on the repository being private to protect it.

questions.explanation: Project-scoped `.mcp.json` is exactly the mechanism designed for this: committing it distributes the server configuration automatically to everyone who clones the repo, while `${JIRA_TOKEN}` expansion means the actual secret is injected from each developer's own environment at runtime rather than stored in version control. Option B requires every developer to manually configure their personal file and hardcodes a live credential into a file that risks being copied or leaked. Option C solves neither stated requirement — every developer still does manual setup, exactly what "without individually configuring it" rules out. Option D commits a real token to version control, which remains in the repository's history indefinitely regardless of the repo's current visibility settings and is exactly the plaintext-commit outcome the requirement excludes.

questions.questionText: A developer-productivity agent connected to a data-warehouse MCP server repeatedly makes exploratory tool calls — "what tables exist," "what columns does this table have" — before it can answer even simple questions about the data, adding latency and consuming context on nearly every session. What MCP feature most directly addresses this discovery overhead?

options.A: Add a `list_tables` meta-tool to the server that returns all table names and schemas when called.
options.B: Expose the table catalog as an MCP resource, giving the agent visibility into what data is available at connection time rather than through exploratory tool calls.
options.C: Reduce the number of query tools in the MCP server by merging related ones together to shrink the discovery surface.
options.D: Add a caching layer that stores the results of previous discovery calls and reuses them across sessions.

questions.explanation: MCP resources exist precisely to expose this kind of content catalog at connection time, so the agent has the schema information available before it ever needs to ask — eliminating the exploratory round trips rather than making them faster or cheaper. Option A still requires a tool call to retrieve the catalog; it reduces the number of exploratory calls from several to one but doesn't remove the discovery step from the agent's workflow the way resources do.
